main_sections.title: Kalakritiyon ka Bharat
main_sections.prose_summary: This section opens with a short introductory passage, 'Hamara Bharat — Incredible India!', which describes Bharat as a land celebrated for its wise and heroic people, diverse culture, rich geography (rivers, lakes, forests, mountains), and unity in diversity. A brief 'Let us discuss' block invites students to reflect on Bharat's identity before moving into the main informational text. The main text, 'Kalakritiyon ka Bharat', is framed as an Ek Bharat, Shreshtha Bharat video conference in which four children from different Indian states each share a traditional art form from their region. Aakansha from Uttarakhand describes Aipan, a folk art of geometric and nature-inspired designs drawn with white rice flour paste on brick-red (geru) walls and floors during family functions and festivals — an exclusively female art form. Priyaranjan from Odisha introduces Dhokra, a 4,000-year-old tribal metal craft using the lost-wax (cire perdue) process: a clay figure is coated in wax, fine details are carved, then covered in clay and fired; the melted wax is replaced by molten brass scrap, which takes the wax's shape after the outer clay layer is removed. Chitra from Kerala describes coconut shell craft, a biodegradable process of cleaning, smoothing, shaping, stabilising, and polishing brown coconut shells into household items such as bowls and jewellery. Balamurali from Andhra Pradesh explains the 400-year-old Kondapalli toy-making tradition: soft local wood is carved in parts, joined with makku (tamarind seed powder and sawdust paste), detailed, and coloured with oil, water-colours, vegetable dyes, or enamel paints. Themes depicted include folk stories, animals, birds, bullock carts, and rural life. A closing informational note describes the Ek Bharat, Shreshtha Bharat programme as a government initiative to promote unity in diversity through state-pairing, mutual cultural exchange, and nation-building.

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Let us read + Let us discuss + Let us think and reflect

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us read the following passage. [Hamara Bharat — Incredible India!] Let us discuss: Answer the following questions.

question_bank.stem: What has Bharat always been known for?
What is attractive about Bharat?
What is special about the fact that the people live here in unison?
What geographical features does the passage mention?
What is the advice given to everyone?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us do these activities before we read. Our country is beautiful and every state has its own uniqueness. The words given below are part of our identity. Complete these words with the correct vowels (A, E, I, O, U).

question_bank.stem: We like to decorate our homes with our f __ l k __ r t. (folkart)
Our c __ l t __ r e consists of our ideas, customs, traditions, values, arts, and languages. (culture)
All of us celebrate our f __ s t __ v __ l s together. (festivals)
We tell stories to our children to teach them about our c __ l t __ r e, h __ s t __ r y, and l __ g __ n d s. (culture, history, legends)
We have our own l __ n g __ a g __ s to talk to each other. (languages)

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us read — Part I. Let us discuss: 1. What is the Ek Bharat, Shreshtha Bharat programme all about? 2. Why is this programme being conducted?

question_bank.stem: What is the Ek Bharat, Shreshtha Bharat programme all about?
Why is this programme being conducted?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss — On the basis of what the speakers said about the art forms, complete the table given below.

question_bank.stem: Complete the table: Name of the child | State | Art form | Things needed. (Akanksha — Aipan; Priyaranjan — Dhokra — clay, brass scrap, wax, fire; Chitra — Kerala — coconut shell craft; Balamurali — Andhra Pradesh — Kondapalli)
question_bank.table:
Name of the child|State|Art form|Things needed
Akanksha|Uttarakhand|Aipan|white rice flour paste, geru
Priyaranjan|Odisha|Dhokra|clay, brass scrap, wax, fire
Chitra|Kerala|Coconut shell craft|coconut shell, sandpaper, wood polish
Balamurali|Andhra Pradesh|Kondapalli|soft wood, makku, colours/dyes

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us think and reflect — I. Fill in the blanks to complete the following sentences.

question_bank.stem: The designs for Aipan are taken from ________________ and ________________.
Dhokra metal craft is very old because it is ________________ old.
A base is needed for coconut shell craft to make it ________________.
Soft wood, ________________ and colours are used to make Kondapalli toys.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us think and reflect — II, III, IV. Answer the following questions.

question_bank.stem: Why does Akanksha's family make Aipan?
In Dhokra, why does wax come out of the small openings?
What is common in the toys made in Balamurali's village and coconut shell craft in Kerala?

listening.transcript_text: Namaste! You must be tired and hungry after listening to so many people. I am Sushil from Jabalpur in Madhya Pradesh. Let me tell you a unique dish of my district. It is called gakkad bharta. First, we make gakkad from wheat dough. Then, we make balls with that dough and roast them on high heat. To make bharta, we roast brinjals and tomatoes. Then we garnish the bharta with chopped onions, coriander, green chillis and salt. This food item is made without frying. To eat it, you simply crumble the gakkad and drizzle it with some bharta and a spoonful of ghee. Come to Jabalpur and try it!

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Listen to the description of gakkad bharta and complete the notes given below. (refer to page 162 for transcript)

question_bank.stem: The town of the speaker : __________________________
The state he belongs to : __________________________
The name of the food item : __________________________
Vegetables roasted for bharta : __________________________

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Now, listen to the description again and complete the flowchart on how to make gakkad bharta.

question_bank.stem: Step 1: To make gakkad, make balls of ___________ dough and ___________ them on high heat.
Step 2: To make bharta, ___________ brinjal and tomatoes and garnish with ___________ onions, coriander, green chillies and salt.
Step 3: To serve, crumble the gakkad, put some ___________ on it and add one spoon of ghee.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Speak about a local art form of your region. It can be any one out of painting, rangoli, metal craft, embroidery, music, dance or drama. Remember to include: the name of the art form, what things do they need for it, how do they make/present it. You may use the words given below when you speak: I know about the art form called … / They need ... / To make it, first ... after that ... next ... then ... at last ...

question_bank.stem: Speak about a local art form of your region (painting, rangoli, metal craft, embroidery, music, dance, or drama). Include the name, what is needed, and how it is made/presented.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You heard children from different states speak about the art forms from their state. Discuss in pairs which art form you liked the most. Now, write a paragraph with five sentences giving your reasons for liking it.

question_bank.stem: Write a paragraph with five sentences giving your reasons for liking the art form you liked most from what the children described.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Priyaranjan, Chitra and Balamurali share how art works of their states are made. They use some words to show the order of making it. Those words are: to begin, first, then, after that, next, finally, at last. Now, use some of these words to complete the paragraph given below. Use one word only once. — 'How I Get Ready for School'

question_bank.stem: 1. ____________ I get up and go to take a shower. 2. ____________, I wear my school dress. 3. ____________, I eat my food. 4. ____________, I wear my shoes. 5. ____________, I pick up my bag and go out of my home.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: The children from different states each speak about one art form from their state. When they speak, they use the first form of the verb (present tense). With he, she and it, -s/-es is used with the verb. Make five sentences with the help of the words given in the table below. Use -s/-es wherever necessary. Two examples are given.

question_bank.stem: Make five complete sentences by combining pronouns (I/You/He/She/It/We/They), verbs (ask/know/tell/draw/make/practise/speak), and sentence parts (how to dance / questions to understand / beautiful flowers / a story every day / very loudly / paintings in free periods / spellings on Friday). Use -s/-es where needed.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Madhubani painting is a traditional art form from Bihar. The artists use natural colours to make them on the walls of their houses. You can also make and use natural colours as given below: yellow from haldi, green from leaves, red from red flowers/geru/sindoor, orange from flowers, blue from neel/indigo powder. Given below is a Madhubani painting. Colour it with natural colours or any other colour.

question_bank.stem: Prepare natural colours using the given materials (haldi, leaves, geru, flowers, indigo powder). Use them to colour the Madhubani painting provided.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read about the Ek Bharat, Shreshtha Bharat programme (p.140). The programme aims to enhance interaction and promote mutual understanding between people of different states/UTs through state/UT pairing.

question_bank.stem: Find out which state your state is paired with under the Ek Bharat, Shreshtha Bharat programme. What cultural practices from that state would you like to learn about?

main_sections.title: The Kites
main_sections.poem_text:
Up in the air
See the kites fly,
Like coloured birds
In the wind-whipped sky.

I wish I were small
And light as air,
I would climb on a kite
And sail up there.

Then I would drift upon
The paper wings,
And hear the songs
That the wild wind sings.

What fun it would be
To look right down,
Over the park
And the rooftops of town.

The people below
Would stand and stare,
And wish they were me
High, high in the air.
main_sections.poem_appreciation_summary: Daphne Lister's poem is a joyful fantasy of flight seen through the imagination of a child watching kites soar on a windy day. The central conceit is the child's wish to become small and light enough to ride a kite, drifting on its paper wings and listening to the wild wind. The poem moves from observation (stanza 1: kites like coloured birds) to desire (stanzas 2–3: climbing aboard, sailing, hearing the wind sing) to imagined triumph (stanzas 4–5: looking down over park and rooftops while people below stare enviously). The tone is exhilarated and playful, with a gentle irony in the final stanza — the child imagines others wishing they were 'high, high in the air.' The dominant literary devices are simile ('Like coloured birds'; 'light as air') and alliteration ('wild wind'; 'wind-whipped'). The repetition of 'high, high' in the closing line emphasises elevation and wonder. The rhyme scheme (ABCB in most stanzas) and the lilting four-line stanzas give the poem the lightness of the kites it describes.

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Let us read + Let us discuss + Let us think and reflect

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us do these activities before we read. I. Answer the following questions and share them with your teacher and classmates: 1. Have you ever flown a kite or seen someone fly it? Where? When? 2. Describe the kite—colour(s), shape, design of tail, any other detail. 3. What did you think when you saw the kite up in the sky?

question_bank.stem: Have you ever flown a kite or seen someone fly it? Where? When?
Describe the kite—colour(s), shape, design of tail, any other detail.
What did you think when you saw the kite up in the sky?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: II. Search the internet for videos on Kite Festivals and watch them with your teacher and classmates. 1. Share the things with your teacher that you saw and what the people were doing. 2. Would you like to participate in this kind of festival? Why? 3. What kind of kite would you like to fly?

question_bank.stem: Share the things you saw in the Kite Festival videos — what were the people doing?
Would you like to participate in this kind of festival? Why?
What kind of kite would you like to fly?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: III. Look at the picture and complete the sentences using the words: windy, kite, flying, birds, sky, looking, has ribbons. IV. Now, think and answer.

question_bank.stem: The weather in the picture is ___________.
The kite is ___________ high in the sky.
The child is ___________ from below.
The tail of the kite ___________.
List two more things that you want to add to the picture.
If I were the child, I would ___________.
If I were the kite, I would ___________.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss — I. Read the poem silently. As you read, mark the given statements as True or False.

question_bank.stem: Mark True or False: 1. The child is looking at the kites. 2. The kites look like birds of different colours. 3. It was a rainy day. 4. The child wants to be like a kite. 5. The child wishes to climb on a kite. 6. The kite is made of cloth and plastic. 7. The child wants to ride the kite. 8. The child knows they would have fun. 9. The child wants to look at the kite from a rooftop. 10. The child would like to look at people down below. 11. The child knows that the people would stare.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss — II. Complete the following sentences about simile in the poem.

question_bank.stem: The kites have been compared to b __ __ __ __ because they ______________. (The poet says kites are 'Like coloured birds')
The child wishes to be as l __ __ __ t as __ __ __. The child wishes this to be able to c __ __ m __ on a kite and fly. (The child wishes to be 'light as air')
Do you think that the use of simile in this poem helps us imagine better when we read the poem? Share your thoughts with your teacher and classmates.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: III. Find a set of words from the poem that begin with the same consonant sound (alliteration). IV. In the last stanza, find the repeated words. V. Study each stanza and underline the rhyming words; circle the end words that do not rhyme.

question_bank.stem: Find words in Stanza 1 that begin with the same consonant sound. Which consonant sound do both words begin with? (Alliteration)
Find words in Stanza 3 that begin with the same consonant sound. Which consonant sound do both words begin with?
Create 4 other sets of words using alliteration.
In the last stanza, the two words that the poet repeats are __________, __________ to tell us that the kite is flying __ __ __ y high.
Study each stanza and underline the rhyming words from the end of each line. Also, circle the end words in the stanzas that do not rhyme.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us think and reflect — I. Read the given lines ('What fun it would be / To look right down / Over the park / And the rooftops of town') and answer the questions.

question_bank.stem: Circle the word that does not share the same feeling as 'fun': joy, excitement, care, happiness.
Which line tells us that the poet was somewhere above?
Choose the scene the child saw (from four picture options showing: a road crossing, rooftops/houses from above, a highway from above, and building facades).

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us think and reflect — II. Answer the following questions.

question_bank.stem: How does the poet describe the kites in the first two lines?
Why does the child say that they have to climb a tree first to get onto the kite?
What are the songs that the child hears when flying atop the kite?
Why did the people look up at the child and stare? If you looked up and saw that, what feelings would you have?
If you were the kite carrying the child high up in the air, what would you tell them?

listening.transcript_text: Hello everyone! I'm happy to share with you some details about the Kite Festival. It is a popular festival in India and it takes place during Makar Sankranti in the month of January. People can even be seen flying kites from their terraces and rooftops and taking part in kite flying competitions. This festival is celebrated as Uttarayan in Western India. The festival gives everyone a chance to look at kites of different shapes, sizes and colours. Gujarat is the main centre of celebrations but Uttarayan is also celebrated in Rajasthan and Telangana. In Karnataka, the Tourism Department has also been organising the Kite Festival once each year. Punjab also celebrates this festival of kites during Baisakhi and Basant Panchami. Kite flying is a tradition that is deep-rooted in Indian culture and the Kite Festival is one that is eagerly awaited. This is also a reason why people across India also fly kites to celebrate Independence Day. Happy kite-flying to all of you!

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Listen about the Kite Festival in India. As you listen, circle the words that are not correct. Then, listen again and write the correct words. (refer to page 163 for transcript)

question_bank.stem: Kite Festival is celebrated during the month of June. [Circle incorrect word and write the correct word]
The International Kite Festival is named Uttarayan in Eastern India. [Circle incorrect word and write the correct word]
Uttarayan is mainly celebrated in Gujarat, but also in Maharashtra and Telangana. [Circle incorrect word and write the correct word]
During the Kite Festival, we can see kites of different shapes, sizes and colours. [True or False?]
In Karnataka, the Tourism Department organises the Kite Festival every year. [Circle incorrect word and write the correct word]
The Punjab region celebrates Basant Panchami and Baisakhi by flying kites. [True or False?]
People of India also enjoy flying kites on Earth Day. [Circle incorrect word and write the correct word]

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Share a kite-flying experience with your classmates. If you have not flown a kite yet, share an experience of watching kites fly. Give details — Where? When? Why? How? Speak in complete sentences.

question_bank.stem: Share a kite-flying experience (or an experience of watching kites fly). Include details: Where? When? Why? How? Speak in complete sentences.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Imagine yourself as a kite. In pairs, first discuss all the following hints: What shape of kite are you? What colours do you have? What other parts of yourself are you happy about? Where are you? Who do you belong to? What activities do you do? Who are your friends? Do you have any message for humans? Now, write a paragraph of about 100 words, with the title—I am a Kite.

question_bank.stem: Write a paragraph of about 100 words, titled 'I am a Kite', imagining yourself as a kite. Include: your shape, colours, parts you like, where you are, who you belong to, your activities and friends, and any message for humans.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Look at the picture of the kite and read what some of its parts are named (Nose, Spine, Cover, Kite line, Tail, Reel). Now, complete the given conversation by filling the blanks with some 'parts of a kite' from above.

question_bank.stem: Bina: I like the long t _ _ _ at the end of my kite. What is your kite like? Saroj: My kite's c _ _ _ _ _ is bright. The s _ _ _ e is black. Bina: Oh wonderful! My kite's l _ _ _ is attached to the r _ _ l that wraps it. Saroj: Did you know that the tip of a kite is called a _ _ s _?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us create a cinquain poem using words in a kite! A cinquain is a five-line poem: Line 1 = one noun (subject), Line 2 = two describing words, Line 3 = three action words, Line 4 = two words describing the action, Line 5 = one synonym for the subject. Example: Kite / pretty, colourful / flying, floating, soaring / quickly, happily / paper-bird. Now, create a cinquain poem yourself on anything you like.

question_bank.stem: Create your own cinquain poem on any topic of your choice, following the five-line structure: (1) noun, (2) two describing words, (3) three action words, (4) two adverbs/descriptors, (5) synonym.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: The poet uses 'fly' and 'drift' to tell us about the kite's movement. I. Circle the words that match with the 'drift' movement: slow, fast, quick, upward, downward, round and round, float. II. Study other words about kite movement (glide, dive, soar, swoop, circle) and describe what type of movement they show. III. The poet has used expressions: 'up in the air; look right down; stay up there; stand and stare'. In pairs, make sentences using these expressions.

question_bank.stem: Circle the words that match with the 'drift' movement. You can choose more than one word: slow / fast / quick / upward / downward / round and round / float.
Study the movement words (glide, dive, soar, swoop, circle) and share what type of movement each shows.
In pairs, make sentences using the expressions: 'up in the air', 'look right down', 'stay up there', 'stand and stare'. Write in your notebooks.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: I. The mention of kites exists in ancient Indian texts. It can be found in the poetry of the thirteenth-century Marathi saint and poet, Namadeva. In his poems or gathas, he called a kite a gudi, and there is a mention that the kites were made from kaagad (paper). II. Have a look at some of the different types of kites (Parafoil, Delta, Rokkaku, Diamond-shaped kite, Box kites). Choose which one is commonly seen. 1. Find out the steps to make a kite. 2. Follow the steps and create your own kite. Bring it to school to show your teacher and classmates. 3. Write a message on the kite.

question_bank.stem: Which type of kite (Parafoil, Delta, Rokkaku, Diamond-shaped, Box kite) is most commonly seen in your area? Choose and discuss why.
Find out the steps to make a kite. Follow the steps and create your own kite. Bring it to school to show your teacher and classmates. Write a message on the kite (e.g., Happy Independence Day!).

main_sections.title: Ila Sachani: Embroidering Dreams with her Feet
main_sections.prose_summary: This inspirational prose narrative tells the story of Ila Sachani, a master of Kathiawar embroidery from Moti Vavdi village, Bhavnagar, Gujarat. Ila was born with non-functional hands in Amreli, Gujarat, into a farmer's family. As a child she watched others draw and play, unable to join in, longing to hold a chalk and draw on a slate. Her mother and grandmother, skilled in Kathiawar embroidery, were determined to help her and taught her to create beautiful embroidered patterns using her feet. Threading a needle was extremely hard, but Ila persevered with support from her family, including her younger brother and sister, and eventually mastered many embroidery styles — not only Kathiawar but also Kachhi, Kashmiri, Lucknawi, and others. Embroidery became a means of self-expression. She initially crafted pieces for family and friends, whose admiration spread the word. When the government office in Surat heard of her, they displayed her work at a state exhibition, where it was purchased enthusiastically. Encouraged, Ila participated in exhibitions at the national level, receiving awards and honours including the President's Medal — recognition not only of her artistry but of her willpower and creativity. Through her skill she achieved financial independence, joy, and the freedom to do what she loved. Her story is presented as proof that challenges, met with focus, hard work, positivity, and perseverance, can be turned into victories. The closing sentence reminds readers that art has the power to transcend physical limitations and touch the soul.

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Let us read + Let us discuss + Let us think and reflect

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us do these activities before we read. I. Look at the given picture and information about Pranav M. Balasubramaniam (from Palakkad, Kerala — born without both hands, excelled as artist, singer, and para-sportsperson). Discuss the questions with your teacher and classmates.

Discuss if these steps can be done with feet, instead of hands. 3. What efforts would be needed if someone has to embroider with their feet? Do you think it is possible?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us think and reflect — I. Read the following lines ('Unlike other children, she could not use her hands in the usual way...') and answer the questions.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us think and reflect — II–VII. Answer the following questions.

question_bank.stem: What creative ways did Ila and her family use to overcome the challenges she faced?

listening.transcript_text: Hello everyone! I'm here today to share some simple information about Kathiawar embroidery. This is a very old form of embroidery. It uses many bright colours and also uses many types of stitches, like the chain stitch. The embroidery is commonly seen on clothes and home decoration items. On clothes, it is seen on children's caps, ghagra skirts and men's traditional shirts. In this embroidery, a lot of mirror work is done by fitting small mirrors along with the stitches. Most commonly, the designs used are geometric shapes like squares and triangles. The design of flowers is also used. There is much more to share but I'll do that some other time. Thank you!

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You will listen to a talk about Kathiawar embroidery. As you listen, circle the correct option. (refer to page 164 for transcript)

question_bank.stem: 1. Form: (i) old  (ii) new
2. Colours: (i) soft  (ii) bright
3. Stitches: (i) running stitch  (ii) chain stitch
4. Seen on: (i) children's caps  (ii) pants
5. Things used: (i) small mirrors  (ii) shapes of buttons
6. Designs: (i) elephants and horses  (ii) squares and triangles

tasks_verbatim.task_text: In pairs, write down two questions you would like to ask Ila Sachani. 1. Look at two examples: 'Where were you born?' 'Did you go to school?' — Yes/No questions use a rising tone at the end. 2. Speak the example questions aloud as a peer activity at least five times. 3. Now, take turns to ask your questions to another classmate. Speak clearly and with the correct rising tone.

question_bank.stem: In pairs, write down two questions you would like to ask Ila Sachani. Practise the rising tone for Yes/No questions. Take turns asking and answering with a classmate.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Collect four/five items (such as flowers, leaves, pins, waste paper, old cards, cardboard boxes, straws, twigs, etc.). Use these items to create any kind of artwork. Now, write a paragraph describing the artwork that you have created. Give a suitable title to your paragraph.

question_bank.stem: Create an artwork using collected items (flowers, leaves, pins, waste paper, cardboard, straws, twigs, etc.). Write a paragraph describing the artwork you have created. Give a suitable title to your paragraph.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: I. Read the words from the text given in Column A. Then, try to understand what they mean by reading the sentences in Column B. Match the word to its correct synonym in Column C. (tiny, wonderful, quiet, task, tough — with synonyms: silent/work/happy/small/difficult/fantastic/dangerous)

question_bank.stem: Match each word in Column A to its correct synonym in Column C. Words: tiny, wonderful, quiet, task, tough. Synonyms to choose from: silent, work, happy, small, difficult, fantastic, dangerous. (Two extra synonyms are given.)
question_bank.table:
Word|Synonym
tiny|small
wonderful|fantastic
quiet|silent
task|difficult
tough|difficult

tasks_verbatim.task_text: II. Unscramble the antonyms of the words in Column A: tiny – E G H U, wonderful – R E E B L I T R, quiet – I Y N S O, task – S I A E M T P, tough – S E Y A. Use these antonyms in sentences of your own.

question_bank.stem: Unscramble the antonyms: 1. tiny – E G H U (H __ __ __) 2. wonderful – R E E B L I T R (T __ R __ __ B ___ E) 3. quiet – I Y N S O (N __ __ __ __) 4. task – S I A E M T P (P __ S T __ __ E) 5. tough – SEYA (E __ __ __).
Use each of the five unscrambled antonyms in a sentence of your own.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: III. Read the sentences from the text and focus on the highlighted words 'lots of' and 'many'. 1. The highlighted words tell us about the quantity — do they show: (i) Unmentioned quantity or (ii) Large quantity? 2. Fill in the blanks to complete an example for each word of quantity: (i) many — countable noun, (ii) how much — uncountable noun, (iii) little — uncountable noun, (iv) few — countable noun, (v) some — countable and uncountable, (vi) some — uncountable noun. Create two sentences for each word of quantity and share them.

question_bank.stem: The highlighted words ('lots of', 'many') tell us about the quantity. Choose what the words show: (i) Unmentioned quantity  (ii) Large quantity.

Fill in the blanks with the correct word of quantity: (i) We saw ______ buses on the road. (countable) (ii) How ______ milk do you want? (uncountable) (iii) There is ______ milk needed. (uncountable) (iv) I need ______ examples. (countable) (v) I have ______ energy left. / She picked ______ flowers. (countable + uncountable) (vi) I ate ______ rice. (uncountable)
Create two sentences for each of the six words of quantity (i)–(vi) and share them with your teacher and classmates.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: 1. Does anyone in your family or neighbourhood do embroidery? Find out what type of embroidery they do and how they learnt it. 2. Try a simple stitch on cloth with the guidance of a family member. 3. Visit the given link to find out about embroidery types from different parts of India. Share with your teacher and classmates which one you liked the most and why. https://indianculture.gov.in/textiles-and-fabrics-of-india/type-of-textile/embroidery

question_bank.stem: Does anyone in your family or neighbourhood do embroidery? Find out what type of embroidery they do and how they learnt it. Share with your class.
Try a simple stitch on cloth with the guidance of a family member.

Visit https://indianculture.gov.in/textiles-and-fabrics-of-india/type-of-textile/embroidery and find out about embroidery types from different parts of India. Share with your teacher and classmates which one you liked the most and why.

main_sections.title: National War Memorial
main_sections.poem_text:
In Bharat's heartland, a grand sight to behold,
The National War Memorial, honours brave stories retold.

With respect and grace, it stands with pride,
Each name etched, each stone, each story to confide.

From battle fields to skies, and to oceans wide,
They stand for Bharat, side by side.

The eternal flame, in silence it glows,
Paying homage to their courage that shows.

Their sacrifice, always in our mind,
In blessed memory, forever remind.

With pride and respect, let's always remember,
At the National War Memorial, their sacrifice forever.
main_sections.poem_appreciation_summary: This reverent poem pays tribute to the National War Memorial in New Delhi, established in February 2019 to honour soldiers who gave their lives for Bharat. The poem moves through space and scale — from the heartland where the memorial stands, to battlefields, skies, and oceans — to convey the breadth of sacrifice. The central image is the eternal flame that 'glows' in silence, a symbol of undying memory and homage. The tone is solemn and patriotic, built on repetition of words like 'pride', 'respect', 'memory', and 'sacrifice'. Key devices include apostrophe (addressing readers collectively: 'let's always remember'), anaphora ('Each name etched, each stone, each story'), and personification (the flame glowing 'in silence'). The rhyme scheme (couplets throughout: behold/retold, pride/confide, wide/side, glows/shows, mind/remind, remember/forever) gives the poem a formal, memorial quality appropriate to its subject. This is a recitation poem — the textbook instructs students to read it aloud, recite it with the class, and memorise it.

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Let us read + Let us discuss + Let us think and reflect

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us do these activities: 1. Read the poem aloud. 2. Recite the poem with your classmates and teacher. 3. Remember the poem.

question_bank.stem: Read the poem aloud, then recite it with your classmates and teacher. Memorise the poem.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: 1. Visit the website of the National War Memorial and learn more about it. https://www.mygov.in 2. Plan a visit to the National War Memorial with your teacher/parents.

question_bank.stem: Visit the National War Memorial website (https://www.mygov.in) and find out three interesting facts about the memorial. Share with your class.
Plan a visit to the National War Memorial with your teacher or parents. Write a short note on what you would like to observe and why.
